entry.title: Re: Extensibility options for SAP Real Estate in S/4HANA...
entry.content:
Dear @Magda_RR,

In Public Cloud only the Contract and Lease Management (CLM) part of classic RE-FX is in scope. Classic RE master data Business Entity, Property, Building, Rental Unit, Pooled Space is not part of Public Edition SAP's strategic solution for that side is Planon Real Estate and Facilities Management for SAP which became an SAP Solution Extension in January 2026. The limited number of Business Contexts and released Badis you see is a consequence of that scoping

More details below:

https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-real-estate-and-facilities-management/ba-p/14310012

On your first point recommended extensibility approaches for FI-impact RE scenarios. The supported model is the standard three tiers Key User Extensibility (Custom Fields, Custom Logic against released Business Contexts), Developer Extensibility (Embedded Steampunk, ABAP Cloud, 3-system landscape) and Side-by-Side on SAP BTP. User-exits and classic modifications are not supported (clean core SAP Note 3578329). In practice for FI impact you work in this order configuration first (expense types, account determination, valuation parameters) then cloud Badis contract-side RECN_CONTRACT_CHECK_ALL / RECN_CONTRACT_SUBSTITUTE / RECE_VALUATION_PARAMETER, FI-side the standard journal entry Badis in the Custom Logic app under Finance / Posting and finally the OData posting API for anything outside a Badi hook

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/3578329

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CLOUD_FOR_REAL_ESTATE/1ccb507ddc6b4191bc1695c2d2995af5/cc59e124fed04627aee1eeaf2e91d53b.html

On your second point released APIs, Badis and events for RE beyond what is in the system. The contract-side Badis you have already found are essentially the delivered toolkit. There is no separate set of RE-specific accounting Badis FI-impact logic goes through the standard FI extension surface. The relevant delivery on the FI side is the Accounting Business Transaction Interface introduced in 2502, with configuration items Define Real Estate Expense Type (106882) and Define Account Determination (106906) and the OData V4 API Real Estate Cost Allocation Accounting Notification Post Journal Entry for posting from external or side-by-side logic, traceable in Manage Accounting Notification (F7289). Release 2508 extended cost settlement across architectural structures, usable/rentable objects and CLM contracts. No RE-specific business events are published on Business Accelerator Hub today

More details below:

https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-contract-and-lease-mgmt-product-amp-strategy-updates-latest-innovation/ba-p/14370862

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/finance-with-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-2502/ba-p/13957394

https://api.sap.com/package/S4HANACloudBADI/overview

https://api.sap.com/products/SAPS4HANACloud/overview

On your third point roadmap and best-practice documentation for customer-specific accounting logic. The canonical reference is Implementing Contract and Lease Management in an SAP S/4HANA Cloud System on SAP Help Portal, together with the relevant scope items (1T6, 21P, 34N, 21Q, 2SA, 2SB, 34O, 2O9, 7EZ, 7G4, 7G5, 21R) and the 2502 / 2508 FI and CLM release notes. On the roadmap side the direction publicly delivered so far is the Accounting Business Transaction Interface and the cost settlement extension anything beyond that is best tracked in SAP Roadmap Explorer

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/70747b45a7ab457b8eafbf4474a20d0c/b7138d1ebe19469a889137bc1abd3ae5.html

On your fourth point the missing Business Context for RE objects as Usable Objects. This follows from the scoping decision above. The classic RE master data layer is not in scope of Public Edition, so a Business Context exposing it for reuse is not delivered. What is exposed Rentable Object, CLM Contract, contract objects is available via released CDS views and OData services visible in ADT and on Business Accelerator Hub. There is no public commitment to deliver an RE master data Business Context if the requirement is concrete and recurring the right channel is SAP Customer Influence campaign 2759

More details below:

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/2759

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Technical Inquiry on Account Differentiation via BADI...
entry.content:
Dear @S0024835231,

BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD is a supported Public Cloud BAdI for Purchase Order Accruals, but its scope is the final Accrual Engine journal entry line item, not the original PO item. SAP confirms that this BAdI can be used to modify journal entry line fields such as G/L account, cost center and profit center for PO Accruals postings

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3068511

For this reason, I would not position BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD as the enhancement point to directly read PO item attributes such as Product Type Group / Policy Type. If the account logic must depend on such PO-level information, this value must first be available in the Accrual Engine context. SAP shows the same design pattern for PO-derived information such as Trading Partner the value is brought into the Accrual Engine using custom field / field mapping logic and then used during Accrual Engine posting

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3256557

For PO Accruals logic that depends on PO item classification or whether an accrual object/item should be created the more relevant enhancement points are the PO Accruals-specific BAdIs for example POAC_ACCRUAL_OBJECT_ITEMS and POAC_PO_ITEM_CLASSIFICATION. SAP confirms POAC_ACCRUAL_OBJECT_ITEMS for influencing PO accrual object items in Public Cloud

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3544598

Conclusion:

BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD can be used to dynamically override the final debit/credit G/L account on Accrual Engine posting lines. It should not be used as the main PO item classification BAdI. For Policy Type / Product Type Group-based account logic the recommended design is to derive or map the policy value into the Accrual Engine context first, and then use BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD only for the final G/L account adjustment

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Acing the SAP Roles and Authorization Design Review — What SAP Checks and How to Prepare
entry.content:
Treat your Non-Prod systems like production. Because, SAP certainly will.

We recently had a security design review done by SAP itself, and we came out with positive feedback on almost all parameters. To be honest, I was a little anxious before the report, but we had been doing everything right, so it went well. I tried to find what SAP checks before the review, but couldn't find much; hence writing this blog. This information will come in handy while prepping for a Design Review. Fair warning — if you follow all of this outside of review season too, your system will be embarrassingly clean. People will start asking what your secret is. Just smile and point them here.

Start taking care of all these things from the QA system onwards. Whatever system, SAP performs review on, they treat it as Prod system and test all parameters, and sometimes it becomes really hard to make the client understand that couple profile assignments to service users/systems users is fine 🙂  particularly the clients who are new to SAP and traditionally had one environment for their ERPs (trust me, I have seen those).

I'll go one by one, divided into 7 sections:

  1. Governance
  2. User Administration
  3. Role Administration
  4. Fiori Launchpad Administration
  5. Custom Transactions
  6. Authorization-based SAP S/4HANA Enterprise Management
  7. Miscellaneous

1. Governance

  1. Authorization Concept Documentation - No matter what phase you are in, always have the documentation updated. Like your user management policy, role design documents -> design and the role naming principles etc. It can be as simple as couple slides, but having that is a must. A good role design document should cover the full picture: what roles exist and what access they give, your naming convention, your list of critical accesses and controls. Make sure you maintain version history, segregated sections for Master, Derived and Composite Roles. My advice: don't create this document for the review. Create it as soon as you start the design, update it as your design evolves, and make sure it's accessible to key stakeholders. That's what SAP wants to see.
  2. Naming Convention - I am not going to say that, you should use ZD or Z_D for derived roles. Use anything, but it has to be consistent. If you've been disciplined about this (e.g., prefixes that distinguish security roles, display roles, composite roles, etc.), you're fine. This one is easy to get right if you define the convention early and enforce it. Don't let people create ad-hoc role names mid-project.

2. User Administration

  1. Master Roles Assigned to Users - Master roles are templates — they should never be directly assigned to users. Only derived roles go on users. SAP checks this explicitly. If your project has been following a proper derivation concept, you'll have no findings here.
  2. Direct Profile Assignments — Especially SAP_ALL and SAP_NEW - SAP looks for users who have profiles assigned directly (outside of PFCG roles), and specifically calls out SAP_ALL and SAP_NEW. In QA environments, teams often give these to BASIS team members while roles are still being built. I would suggest a better one, until you have the matured roles for basis, create one role from SAP_ALL template, deactivate Sec related objects. May be call this Y*SAP_ALL*MINUS_SECURITY, but use it until you have enough inputs to create one support/FF role. A word of caution on interface users; these are often the ones that get forgotten. SAP_ALL on a system or service user is actually more dangerous than on a dialog user, because it opens the door for RFC-based attacks. Ideally you should have minimum privileged roles for them, but in the initial phases, when you don't have enough inputs, use the same role (SAP_ALL minus security). You can use STUSERTRACE for building interface user roles. Refer - STUSERTRACE users 

  3. Expired / Terminated Users with Role Assignments - SAP checks for user IDs that are expired or terminated but still have active role assignments. Locking a user is not enough, remove the role assignments too. It demonstrates that your offboarding process is complete, not just half-done. Have an admin process documented for this and make sure it's actually running even in non-prod systems.

  4. Reference User Type - SAP checks if you are using Reference users assigned to anyone without proper reason and documentation. I am sure, noone uses it, but if you still have any use case, document it or rather use General roles assigned to all composites.

3. Role Administration - This section typically has the most findings in any review. Most are avoidable if you build good habits from the start.

  1. Transaction Ranges in Roles  

    Never use transaction ranges (like MM* or FI*) in PFCG role menus. The reason is simple —> if a new transaction gets created within that range later, users automatically get access to it without any approval or awareness. Always add individual t-codes.

  2. Transactions Not Maintained via Menu 

    Every t-code in a role should be added through the Menu tab in PFCG, not manually inserted into the authorization data. This maintains the relationship between the t-code and its authorization objects. If you remove a t-code from the menu later, the corresponding objects get cleaned up properly. If you bypass the menu, that cleanup doesn't happen — you're left with orphaned objects giving access nobody asked for.

  3. Non-Existing Transactions in Roles

    SAP checks for t-codes in roles that don't actually exist in the system. These are usually leftovers from ECC role copies where certain transactions don't exist in S/4HANA, or from template roles that were never cleaned up. Remove the ghost tcodes once and for all (even in the non production system roles).

  4. Manual Objects in Roles

    This is a big one. Every authorization object in a role should come from SU24 proposals; triggered by adding a t-code via the menu. If you manually add objects directly in the authorization data tab, SAP loses the ability to trace the relationship between that object and the transaction that needs it. Practical Consequence is if you ever remove that t-code, the manually added objects stay behind and keep giving access. And during upgrades or SU24 changes, those manual objects don't get updated automatically. 

  5. Org. Access Maintained at Node Level

    Organizational field values (Company Code, Plant, Sales Org, etc.) should be maintained through the proper derivation mechanism, via the "Organizational Data" tab in PFCG. Not directly in the authorization data. If you edit them at the node level, the values get corrupted. Look for such values, fix it.

  6. Incorrect / Non-Existing Org Values -

    SAP also looks for org values in roles that don't exist in the system anymore. Old company codes, retired plants, restructured sales orgs. These don't break anything immediately. Run a periodic cleanup and remove obsolete org values. A quick comparison of values in AGR_1252 against the actual org data would give you the culprits.

  7. Display Roles with Maintenance Access

    If a role's name contains DISP or REPORT, SAP expects it to have only display/read access. If it also has write activities such as create, change, delete, post etc., that's a problem.

  8. Not Generated Roles

    After any change to a role, it needs to be regenerated in PFCG. An ingenerated role means users assigned to it get no access at all.

  9. Roles with Open Authorizations

    Open authorization nodes are objects in the role with no values maintained, means they don't grant access, but they create confusion and make roles hard to audit. Deactivate the nodes you don't need, fill in the values for the ones you do, and regenerate.

  10. Inconsistent Master-Derived Roles

    SAP checks if derived roles have diverged from their master. Different field values, or worse, the master role no longer exists. This breaks the whole point of the derivation concept and can lead to excess access being granted unintentionally.

4. Fiori Launchpad Administration - SAP gives a lot of attention to FIORI in S4HANA secuirty design reviews. This area surprised me as it felt more like UX administration than security. But from SAP's perspective - its both UX and Security.

  1. Use of FIORI Groups

    The FIORI groups concept is obsolete/deprecated as of SAP S/4HANA 2021. So, if you have older groups created, try to shift to Catalogs, but still you can justify the groups. But dont create any new group at all.

  2. Unused Catalogs and Groups

    Any catalog not assigned to a PFCG role is invisible to users. It's just dead weight in the system. Clean them up or assign them. SAP is firmly in the Spaces and Pages world now. If you're still using groups, plan your migration. Don't go live with a legacy Fiori structure.

  3. Enterprise Search Authorizations

    One of the most overlooked items :). People often end up maintaining S_ESH_CONN as * for all the fields. This is both a performance and Security issue. Try to maintain the proper template name and connection ID.

  4. SAP Standard Business Catalogs and Roles

    Never assign SAP standard Business Roles or catalogs directly to users or PFCG roles. Always copy them into your customer namespace, adjust to your org needs and then assign. This is both a security and a maintainability best practice.

  5. Orphaned Catalogs and Groups

    Catalogs or groups that exist but aren't assigned anywhere are just clutter. Clean them unless you have an active ongoing project reason to keep them. The same applies to orphaned tiles in Fiori Pages, where tiles pointing to catalog entries that no longer exist. These confuse users and clutter your Fiori setup.

  6. System Alias Consistency

    Fiori apps rely on system aliases to find the correct backend. If an alias is in error or has warnings, the associated OData service can't register, and the app simply won't work. Check this using SM59 or /UI2/GW_SYS_ALI and fix any discrepancies. Don't let alias issues go into production; they're hard to diagnose for end users.

  7. Granularity of Catalogs

    SAP doesn't want you to create one catalog per app. Even 2-3 is considered a bad design. Atleast 5 is a good number, if you don't want to get highlighted 🙂

  8. Service Activation

    Every Fiori app depends on an activated OData service. If the service isn't active or is in error, the app simply won't load, no error message that makes sense to the user, just a broken tile. Review all roles and catalogs and check service status. Implement a periodic control to catch regressions after transports.

  9. OData Metadata Cache

    Schedule report /UI5/UPD_ODATA_METADATA_CACHE. In test systems, hourly is fine. In production, once per night is the recommendation. This directly affects how fast Fiori apps load. It also needs to be run after importing transports, implementing SAP notes, or changing a service. Make it part of your go-live checklist.

  10. SAP Menu Entry Cache

    Similarly, run /UI2/EAM_BUILD_CACHE to cache SAP menu entries in the App Finder. Without it, the App Finder either loads very slowly or times out. If the report isn't available in your system, check whether you need to install the required SAP_UI patch level or SAP Note 2545066.

5. Custom Transactions

  1. Unused Custom Transactions

    SAP checks for Y* and Z* transactions that exist in the system but aren't in any role menu. These are typically leftovers from earlier development, retired features, or transactions that were built but never formally deployed. Lock or remove the ones you don't need. Unused custom t-codes sitting in the system are a quiet compliance risk, they can be executed by anyone with broad access, and nobody is tracking them.

  2. Custom Transactions Not Maintained in SU24

    Well, Well, Well, this is the item, which I keep iterating to ABAP teams and the client. Every custom program should have an AUTHORITY-CHECK statement, and not just for the namesake, it should check the most relevant Auth objects, for example, if a custom report is built to get the sales orders report, it should check the objects which are checked by VA03 tcode, if not all, at least few of those. Now, you can remediate this one by just adding a custom obj check in SU24 and roles, but it wont remediate the underlying vulnerability. Ideal flow should be Appropriate checks in Program --> maintained in SU24 --> Added in roles --> Added in GRC risk library (functions). 

6. Authorization-Based SAP S/4HANA Enterprise Management (Licensing) - 

This is the section most teams don't think about, while creating roles. But SAP does. Certain authorization objects automatically trigger Full User Equivalent (FUE) licensing, meaning any user with those objects in their roles gets charged at the highest license tier. The objects that typically come up are things like S_CALENDAR, S_PSE_ADM, S_TRANSPRT, S_USER_GRP, and S_SECPOL etc. 

Refer note - 3113382 - Authorization-based SAP S/4HANA User Simulation / FUE Projection.

7. Miscellaneous — Security Profile Parameters - SAP checks quite a few security, rfc, password related parameters if you have an on-premise system. But if you are on RISE, most of these are taken care and SAP checks these parameters -

auth/check/calltransaction — Controls whether authority checks are enforced during CALL TRANSACTION statements. Recommended value is 3. If it's not set correctly, authorization checks can be silently bypassed in background and batch processes.

auth/object_disabling_active — Controls whether authorization checks can be globally switched off for selected objects. Recommended value is N. If this is active, someone with the right access can effectively turn off security for entire authorization objects system-wide. It should be N in any production system.

auth/rfc_authority_check — Controls how authentication is enforced for RFC function module calls. Recommended value is 6. Anything lower means remote function calls can be made without proper authorization checks — essentially leaving a door open for unauthenticated access via RFC. This one is often set to a lower value during development for convenience, and then forgotten. Get your Basis team to set it to 6 before go-live.

 

Final thought

The review itself is not the hard part. The hard part is the daily discipline during the project like maintaining SU24, following the menu-based role building approach, keeping org values clean, not letting SAP_ALL sit on users "temporarily". These are all habits, and they're much easier to build early than to fix under review pressure.

Treat your QA system like production. If you won't, SAP certainly will.

entry.title: Re: Source Layer of “Your session has expired” Message in...
entry.content:
I suspect this is your OAuth access and refresh tokens. 
You can review the configuration on SAP Mobile Services cockpit , in your app security section. These settings should also reflect the time set on your iDP provider. 

When the OAuth Token expires it uses the refresh token to retrieve a new OAuth token, however after the refresh token expires it forces the User to log back in via the iDP to get new tokens..

entry.title: Guidance Needed for SAP Learning Journey and SAP Security Career Path
entry.content:
Dear SAP Learning and Community Team,

I hope you are doing well.

I am currently studying for a Bachelor’s degree in Cybersecurity and IT at Rutgers University, and I recently received access to SAP Learning resources and certifications through my university opportunity.

I am very interested in becoming SAP certified and building a future career related to SAP, cybersecurity, enterprise systems, and emerging technologies. I am also actively learning and exploring AI, and I am very interested in understanding how AI, cybersecurity, and SAP technologies may connect in future enterprise environments.

However, I am currently a complete beginner in SAP and do not yet have prior knowledge of ERP systems, SAP modules, accounting concepts, FI/CO, MM, business processes, T-codes, or SAP terminology.

As I started exploring the SAP Learning portal, I found there are many learning journeys, modules, and specialization paths, which made it difficult for me to understand where I should begin and what sequence I should follow.

Since my academic background is in Cybersecurity and IT, I would sincerely appreciate guidance on the best SAP learning roadmap that aligns with my studies and long-term career goals. I would especially value recommendations related to SAP Security, GRC, governance, access management, enterprise security, AI-related SAP technologies, or other cybersecurity-relevant SAP areas.

I would be very grateful if your team could kindly guide me regarding:

1. Which beginner learning journeys I should start with from scratch
2. How I should learn ERP concepts, SAP basics, navigation, and business processes step by step
3. Whether I should first learn FI/CO, MM, T-codes, Internal Orders, Profit Centers, and SAP fundamentals before moving into SAP Security
4. The recommended sequence of courses and learning journeys for someone with a Cybersecurity and IT background
5. Which SAP certifications would be most relevant and valuable for my future career goals
6. Which learning journey could eventually lead me toward SAP Security or SAP GRC certification paths
7. Whether there are SAP learning paths that combine AI, enterprise systems, and cybersecurity concepts

My goal is to follow a proper structured roadmap instead of randomly selecting courses without understanding the correct direction.

I would sincerely appreciate your guidance and recommendations.

Thank you very much for your time and support.

Kind regards,
Inaya

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Discovery Center: From Inspiration to Guidance
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is here! This year’s event brings innovations and updates to SAP technologies like never before. Not only are the technologies evolving rapidly, but the business impact we will see across industries is equally impressive.

For inspiration and guidance on translating your vision from what’s possible with these technologies to how you can achieve maximum results, SAP Discovery Center is your resource. For evaluating new technologies or expanding current solutions, the Discovery Center resources help you move from exploration to implementation faster and with less guesswork.

If you are new to SAP Discovery Center, watch this quick overview video to get started.

SAP Discovery Center Home Page

The latest release brings new enhancements to Discovery Center, designed to speed up your journey from simple exploration to impactful adoption.

Let’s dive into what’s new for SAP Discovery Center.

New SAP Business Data Cloud Service Catalog

Explore SAP Business Data Cloud Services in one central place. Discovery Center has expanded its catalog offerings with a new catalog for SAP BDC Services. Get instant access to missions, roadmaps, and guidance for managing SAP and non-SAP data. Review transparent pricing and practical examples of how these services solve your business challenges. You can easily move from initial discovery to solution adoption with expert guidance and direction.

SAP Business Data Cloud Service Catalog

 Explore the SAP Business Data Cloud Services Catalog and start a mission.

New Maturity Assessments for SAP Business AI

Introduced at last year’s SAP TechEd, Maturity Assessments are comprehensive technical evaluations that transform uncertainty into a prioritized investment roadmap. In just 10-15 minutes, you'll identify platform or AI maturity gaps and benchmark against industry peers.

SAP BTP Adoption Framework Landing Page

We have since expanded the portfolio with the new Line of Business AI assessments for domain-specific insights, plus an AI Maturity Calculator developed with IDC. The AI Maturity Calculator benchmarks your AI readiness against your industry, helping you understand where you stand across three maturity levels: Observing, Advancing, and Leading. 

Learn more about the AI execution gap and current IDC research in this blog post: Most companies have an AI strategy. Here’s what the top 8% do differently.

Take a free assessment and try the AI Maturity Calculator.

A Refreshed User Experience

With a fresh home page design, updated icons and colors, SAP Discovery Center makes site navigation and orientation easier. Each catalog features its own color scheme and related icons.

Mission Catalog

Experience the new design on the home page and in the Mission Catalog.

New AI Offerings

AI Agent: Project Billing Verification Agent (Beta)

The Project Billing Price Verification Agent in the Manage Project Billing application of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition helps billing specialists quickly identify mismatches between agreed prices and billing amounts.

AI Feature: SAP Digital Manufacturing, Joule

The conversational search feature with Joule provides faster, smoother, contextual access to SAP Help Portal content summarized in a clear answer to the question.

AI Feature: SAP Integrated Product Development, Joule

Joule Basic Navigation to the initial page of each IPD application, such as “Open My Collaboration app”, “Open Manage Specification app” etc.

New Missions

Activate Joule for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition

This mission guides you through the setup of SAP Joule with your SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud instance. Joule revolutionizes how you interact with SAP business systems, making every touchpoint count and every task simpler.

Activate free SAP Build - Test, Demo and Development for SAP Partners

This mission helps SAP Partners to activate the free SAP Build test, demo, and development (TDD) licenses. SAP provides free access to SAP Build test, demo, and development (TDD) licenses for all SAP partners. This enables partners to innovate on SAP Build up to fair usage limits for customer workshops, proofs of concepts, building accelerators.

Partner Mission: High Availability Architecture for the UX/HANA Cloud Business Critical apps - (clean core)

When designing SAP BTP User Experience services, it is imperative the architecture has resilience in the case of service disruption, especially when the applications could be business critical. This mission provides option(s) to architect a high availability solution across two providers.

SAP BTP Guidance Framework Becomes SAP BTP Adoption Framework

Same expert guidance with a sharper focus on getting you from planning to production faster.

Get started with the SAP BTP Adoption Framework assets.

Become a SAP Discovery Center Insider

Pro tip: Stay up to date with what’s new in SAP Discovery Center (features and content) via the ‘What’s New & Updates’ page.

What's New & Updates

Recommended Sessions at ASUG/SAP Sapphire

Your platform journey: Maturity assessment and adoption best practices | BTP1549

Explore the new maturity assessment that benchmarks your current state and uncovers optimization opportunities, paired with the proven platform adoption framework for SAP BTP that guides implementation.  

🗓️ Add to schedule

Real-life stories: turning missions into values with SAP Discovery Center | BTP2032

See how Agilent Technologies and Sutherland Global Services have turned ideas into production-ready solutions with SAP Business Technology Platform using missions in SAP Discovery Center. Hear them share their experience from real use cases, practical implementation steps, and key lessons learned, so you can start delivering business value fast.

🗓️ Add to schedule

Use cases to maximize your platform investment | BTP1344

Unlock the next level of value in SAP's platform by exploring high-impact use cases for automation, app extensions, and agentic AI that can be activated using your entitlements in SAP BTP, including the SAP Build solution. Through practical scenarios, see how SAP's platform can help accelerate innovation and turn business challenges into business opportunities.

entry.title: Re: Introducing Expanded Usage of the Reference Capability Model
entry.content:
Hi @SreelathaN.

I'm a big fan of the Reference Business Architecture that SAP has developed and our business embraced it. I’m a little unclear on the use of the term “Reference Capability Model” and the attached spreadsheet in your post.

I’m familiar with both the SAP Reference Business Capability Model and the proposed SAP Reference Technical Capability Model, so I’d appreciate clarification on what is meant here. Is the “Reference Capability Model” intended to represent a combination of these two layers?

The spreadsheet attached to the post introduces a number of Business Domains that don’t align with the Reference Business Capability Model as it currently appears across SAP tooling. In particular, the items highlighted in yellow (in my image below of that sheets Business Domains) don’t seem to exist within the standard model I’m familiar with e.g. Application Development and Automation, Artificial Intelligence, Data and Analytics, Integration, IT Administration, IT Security, Solution Characteristics are not part of the model I'm familiar with. Also, the Business Domain "IT Management" is missing from the spreadsheet.

If these additional domains are indeed being incorporated, it raises a concern around mutual exclusivity, which is a core principle of a well-structured capability model. For example, IT Security already appears to be comprehensively covered within the Governance, Risk, and Compliance domain (e.g., Cyber Security Management, Data Privacy Management, Data Protection, Risk Management, Controls Management, Access Governance, Authentication Management, Identity Management, etc.). Introducing it as a separate domain would create overlap and ambiguity.

Could you please clarify how these additional domains are intended to fit within the model, and whether the expectation is to maintain strict mutual exclusivity across the taxonomy?

entry.title: Re: NDC Converter 2.0: Automating Your Journey from Busin...
entry.content:
Very good explanation. I would like to understand more from an enterprise implementation perspective and how this works practically in real customer landscapes.

  1. When converting Webi reports to SAC, how much of the existing report logic, variables, calculations, prompts, and business rules can realistically be converted by the NDC Converter? Does the tool only migrate report structures/metadata, or can it also preserve underlying business logic?
  2. In scenarios where the current reporting source is SAP ECC (with SLT/HANA sidecar dependencies) and the client is evaluating future architectures such as:
  • ECC → HANA Cloud
  • ECC → Datasphere
  • Datasphere → Webi (interim coexistence)
  • Datasphere → SAC (strategic direction)

how does the NDC Converter help clients understand and assess:

  • source tables/views
  • object dependencies
  • semantic layer impacts
  • migration complexity
  • interim coexistence strategies?
  1. From a long-term enterprise architecture perspective, how can organizations ensure that migrated reports continue to function properly after semantic and source-system transformations (ECC → Datasphere/HANA Cloud/S4)? Practically, how is this managed without causing major business disruption?

Current customer scenario:
The client currently uses SAP ECC as the primary reporting source for a large portion of their Webi reporting landscape. Architecture and modernization decisions are currently in progress, and we are evaluating the best future reporting approach for the client.

The client’s long-term strategy is BTP-centric, and current discussions include:

  • Datasphere as semantic/governed data layer
  • SAC as strategic analytics platform
  • possible interim Webi coexistence
  • evaluation of HANA Cloud stabilization approaches
  • BDC and Databricks enterprise considerations

We are also assessing whether:

  • Datasphere → Webi
    or
  • Datasphere → SAC

would be the better interim and long-term strategy.

In this scenario, how does the NDC Converter realistically fit into the migration strategy? Can it significantly reduce effort for migrating 2400+ Webi reports toward SAC/Datasphere-based architecture, especially in enterprise ECC-centric landscapes?

Appreciate your thoughts, practical experiences, and recommendations.

Thanks,

Vishala.

versus what still requires:

The client’s long-term direction is BTP-centric, with discussions around:

In this type of enterprise landscape, how does the NDC Converter realistically fit into the migration strategy? Can it significantly reduce migration effort for large ECC-centric Webi environments while still supporting phased modernization and minimizing business disruption?

Thanks,

Vishala

entry.title: Re: Business Volume Report and Settlement are not in sync
entry.content:
Hi Martin,

It's a standard behavior. Billing date in the billing document is the basis for business volume determination. But for settlement, it's the combination of billing date and accounting document clearing status. Accruals status will be in Blocked status till the time the billing document is paid. After the billing document is paid, the status will move to released.

Regards

Hari

entry.title: SAC Planning Master Data Mapping for Cost Category (Major/Minor) based on Usage × Account
entry.content:
Background / Current Setup

We are using SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Planning with an import model.

There is an existing source planning model already in use. For operational reasons, we do not want to redesign or modify the source model directly. Instead, we refresh a separate target planning model for analysis using Data Action – Cross Model Copy (manual trigger is acceptable).

Our fact data includes these dimensions:

  • Usage Code
  • Account Code

For reporting, we need additional classification dimensions:

  • Cost Minor Category
  • Cost Major Category (parent level of Cost Minor)

The classification is defined by an Excel mapping table:

  • (Usage Code, Account Code) → Cost Minor Category → Cost Major Category

We also have Data Integration in SAC, but our tenant does not provide the ETL-style Data Flows feature.

Requirements

  1. Populate classification dimensions
  • The combination Usage Code × Account Code uniquely determines the cost classification.
  • After refreshing data into the target model, we want to populate the Cost Minor Category dimension based on that two-key mapping.
  • Cost Major Category should be available via a Major → Minor hierarchy.
  1. Story reporting
  • In Stories, we want to use Cost Major Category as an axis and drill down to Cost Minor Category.
  • Therefore, Cost Major/Minor must exist as dimensions (not just properties).
  1. Refresh operation After the source model is updated, we want a manual process that:
  • runs Cross Model Copy to refresh the target model, and
  • then applies the mapping so the Cost Category dimensions are filled correctly.

Key Challenges / Questions

  1. Recommended modeling approach Because the mapping depends on the combination of two dimensions, it cannot be modeled consistently as a property on Usage Code alone or Account Code alone.
  • Is it recommended/required to create a composite key dimension such as UsageAccountKey = Usage Code + Account Code?
  • Or is there a better SAC Planning modeling pattern to represent a two-key classification?
  1. How to apply the mapping without Data Flows Since Data Flows are not available in our tenant, we cannot perform an ETL-style join in SAC Data Integration.
  • What is the recommended way to store the Excel mapping table in SAC (e.g., as a dimension, a small mapping model, a dataset, etc.)?
  • Can a Data Action (or Advanced Formulas / Data Action steps) perform a lookup to populate a dimension (Cost Minor Category) based on (Usage Code, Account Code)?
  1. Best practice architecture with Cross Model Copy Given a process centered on Cross Model Copy:
  • Should the mapping be applied before the copy (source side), after the copy (target side), or via an intermediate model?
  • What is the best-practice approach to keep the target model reportable with stable Major → Minor drill-down?

What We Are Looking For

A recommended architecture / best practice for SAC Planning import models to:

  • enrich fact data with a classification dimension derived from two keys,
  • support hierarchy-based aggregation and drill-down in Stories,
  • while keeping the source model unchanged and using Cross Model Copy for refresh.

entry.title: Re: Guidance on calling S4 OData API from SAP IS using OA...
entry.content: Thanks Tom. Yeah it looks like we can just configure OAuth for a user account (which already can be used to access the API using Basic Auth) and use a two step process to access the API. Since we need to store and pass the username and password in the first step, it is not really adding any extra security :(. The only advantage I can see is that if we have multiple API calls within the iFlow, the subsequent calls can use the token derived in the start, instead of passing the username and password for each call. This probably increases the security level on the subsequent calls.

entry.title: 08.SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation - Data Migration Key areas and Framework
entry.content:
This is part of an ongoing blog series on SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation. In this post, we are going to talk about the key areas of the Data Migration workstream. Data migration is one of the most complex workstreams in the SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation project. It is often the primary factor that determines whether an implementation is a resounding success or a mess.

I have covered all critical areas at a high level but will write a detailed blog on the critical section with all details.

 

 

 

1) Data Migration Scope : When initiating the Data Migration workstream, the highest priority is to finalize the Data Migration Scope. This is a critical foundational step, as the defined scope directly influences migration complexity, effort, resource requirements, sequencing, and the overall project timeline.

Key areas that play a critical role in scope definition include:

  • Existing system architecture and source systems for data
  • Modules and business processes in scope
  • Data sources and data ownership
  • Historical data retention and migration requirements
  • Compliance, audit, and regulatory considerations
  • Reporting and analytics requirements
  • Data quality and data volume considerations
  • Integrations and downstream dependencies
  • Security and access requirements

2) Data Migration Tools :Identifying the right migration tool is a critical success factor for the Data Migration workstream. The selection of the migration approach and tooling largely depends on the source systems, data structure, transformation requirements, data volume, and overall integration landscape.

Based on the Customer’s existing systems and migration requirements, the project team must evaluate and select the most appropriate migration tools and frameworks. The chosen toolset should support efficient extraction, transformation, validation, and loading of data while ensuring data quality, traceability, and compliance requirements are met.

SAP provides Info Porter Tools to migrate Foundation, Org and Employee data  from the below systems to SF EC. This tool works really great for data migration loads.

  • SAP S/4HANA HCM
  • SAP ERP HCM

3) Data Migration Team - SI Skillset and Customer Resources availability: Data Migration requires strong commitment and dedicated participation from both the Customer and the System Integrator (SI). Data migration activities, such as data mapping, data validation, data cleansing, reconciliation, and testing, are highly business-driven and require deep knowledge of the Customer’s existing systems, data, and processes.

At the same time, it is equally important to ensure the SI provides experienced Data Migration consultants who can offer the right guidance, proven methodologies, and strong delivery ownership.

4) Data Migration Requirements and Mapping workbook ownership: Capturing Data Migration requirements is one of the first and most critical activities during the Realization phase. A well-defined migration requirement and mapping framework establishes the foundation for successful data transformation, validation, and loading activities throughout the project lifecycle.

Defining migration requirements requires close coordination and collaboration among multiple stakeholders, including:

  • SuccessFactors (SF) Functional Resources
  • Data Migration (DM)  Team

Since migration requirements evolve throughout the project, it is important to clearly define ownership, governance, and update processes for the Data Migration Mapping Workbook from the beginning.

5) Data Migration Build and Unit Testing:  Identifying the right team for the Data Migration workstream is critical to the success of the overall implementation. The Data Migration team must have a strong understanding of the SuccessFactors (SF) Data Model, object relationships, data dependencies, and migration sequencing requirements.

At a minimum, key resources assigned to the project should have prior experience in SuccessFactors Data Migration and hands-on knowledge of:

  • SuccessFactors Employee Central data structures
  • Foundation Objects and MDF Objects
  • Effective-dated data handling
  • Load sequencing and dependency management
  • SAP migration tools and templates
  • Data transformation and validation techniques

6) Mock Cycles Planning:  Because Data Migration is one of the most complex and risk-prone workstreams, ne of the key success factors for migration readiness is defining the appropriate number of mock migration cycles before the Production load. More mocks generally provide higher-quality data, but they also increase project timelines. The required number depends on customer complexity, global footprint, and the project deployment approach. I strongly recommend 3 mock cycles with a full load prior to the production load

The number of mock cycles should be determined based on several factors, including:

  • Customer complexity
  • Global footprint and localization requirements
  • Number of countries/business units in scope
  • Volume of historical data
  • Data quality maturity of legacy systems
  • Complexity of integrations and downstream systems
  • Deployment and rollout approach
  • Compliance and reporting requirements

7) Instance Strategy: A dedicated SuccessFactors (SF) instance is required to migrate data, and the SF instance cannot be used for any other purpose during data loads. At the same time, ensuring that the source system (e.g., SAP S/4HANA) is available and that production data has been copied is critical. This must be properly planned as part of the instance strategy for each mock cycle, including which systems will be used. If necessary, an additional SF instance should be provided.

8)Entry and exit criteria for each mock:  For each mock load, we must clearly define the entry criteria required to initiate the exercise, as well as the exit criteria that determine successful completion. As part of the data migration workstream, each activity should also be assigned defined entry criteria and timelines. Without these clearly established controls, there is a high risk of delays and misalignment across dependent activities.

9)Data Scrambling: Protecting sensitive data and coming up with the right data scrambling approach is key. We typically do not migrate real email addresses, national IDs, compensation details, or payment details during Mock loads. Depending on the usage of mock load data, we may include or exclude sensitive data.

If sensitive data is not required during mock loads, we scramble data in the source system or in SuccessFactors before granting user’s access. Partner tools are available to support data scrambling, and some customers also have built-in tools to do so.

 10) Data  Validation Tools and Approach: Data validation requires significant effort. Identifying the right tools—both internal and external—that enable business teams to continuously validate data can greatly accelerate processes and reduce risk. In addition, establishing a dedicated data validation team during mock load or testing phases can significantly improve data quality and further minimize risks before production deployment.

11) Data Cleansing Approach: Data cleansing effort during migration depends primarily on two factors: the quality of the source system data and the number of years of history included in the migration scope. In top-of-stack migrations, effort is typically lower. However, when defining the migration scope, it is critical to recognize that expanding historical data coverage significantly increases cleansing complexity due to accumulated inconsistencies, missing standards, and legacy data issues.

12) Data Archival Strategy: Once you are live with SAP SuccessFactors HCM, we do not bring over all historical data. However, we still need historical data to support different requirements such as reporting, regulatory requirements, rehire processes, and tenure calculations..etc. The right data archival strategy must be defined based on the legacy HR system’s role after SuccessFactors goes live. Some partner tools provide seamless access to historical data from the SuccessFactors system.

13) System freeze strategy during Prod Loads: A proper strategy should be defined during production data loads, including how source system transactions will be governed during production loads. Depending on production load, timelines, and business impact, we carefully plan soft and hard freeze periods. We also define a delta migration approach to minimize disruption.

The InfoPorter tool supports a delta approach when migrating data from SAP S/4HANA HCM or SAP ERP HCM. If you are migrating data from other HCM systems, you should define the appropriate strategy based on system capabilities and project requirements

14) Production Load : Define production load timelines. We usually plan around three weeks to complete production load activities, including data load, validation, and sign-off. However, the timeline may vary depending on complexity. The goal is to achieve a 99.99% validation success rate, which serves as the exit criterion for production load completion.

 

Below is the High-Level SAP SuccessFactors Data Migration Framework cover all key areas in one picture :

entry.title: WalkMe in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
The Missing Layer Between Implementation and Real Adoption

Most organizations believe a project is successful once SAP SuccessFactors goes live.

In reality, go-live is only the beginning.

The real challenge starts when thousands of employees, managers, HR teams, and business users begin using the system daily. That is where many organizations struggle — not because the software is weak, but because users are overwhelmed, confused, or dependent on support teams for even simple activities.

This is exactly the gap WalkMe is designed to solve.

WalkMe is not just a training tool.
It is not simply a help widget.
And it is definitely not another documentation platform.

WalkMe acts as an intelligent digital layer that sits on top of enterprise applications and helps users perform tasks successfully in real time.

Instead of teaching users before they enter the system, WalkMe teaches them inside the system — exactly when they need help.

That shift changes everything.

Why Enterprise Systems Often Fail After Go-Live

Organizations spend months implementing SAP SuccessFactors:

  • Workshops
  • Configurations
  • Testing
  • Integrations
  • Cutover planning
  • Training sessions

Yet after deployment, common issues immediately appear:

  • Employees forget processes
  • Managers avoid self-service
  • HR teams receive repetitive tickets
  • Users enter incorrect data
  • New releases confuse people
  • Adoption remains low

The technology works.

But the users struggle.

Most companies assume training will solve this problem.


Unfortunately, after traditional training:

  • People forget.
  • Employees attend sessions weeks before they actually perform transactions. By the time they use the system, much of the learning is already lost.

 

WalkMe changes this model completely.

What Makes WalkMe Different?

WalkMe transforms enterprise applications from “systems users must learn” into “systems that teach users while working.”

Imagine an employee opening SAP SuccessFactors for the first time.

Instead of searching through PDFs or contacting HR, the system itself says:

  • “Click here.”
  • “Enter this information.”
  • “This field means this.”
  • “You missed a mandatory step.”
  • “Here’s what happens next.”

The application becomes self-guided.

This creates a smoother, more confident user experience without interrupting daily work.

The Core Features That Make WalkMe Powerful

  1. Real-Time Guidance Inside the Application

WalkMe provides interactive walkthroughs directly on the screen.

Users no longer need to switch between:

  • training documents,
  • support emails,
  • videos,
  • or help portals.

The learning happens inside the workflow itself.

For SAP SuccessFactors users, this is especially valuable during:

  • onboarding,
  • performance reviews,
  • compensation cycles,
  • recruitment activities,
  • and employee self-service transactions.
  1. SmartTips That Prevent Errors Before They Happen

Most enterprise mistakes happen because users are unsure what a field means.

WalkMe adds contextual guidance beside fields and buttons so users understand:

  • what to enter,
  • why it matters,
  • and what format is required.

This improves both confidence and data quality simultaneously.

  1. Automation of Repetitive Activities

Many enterprise tasks involve repetitive clicks and navigation steps.

WalkMe can automate parts of these actions, reducing unnecessary effort and saving time for users.

The impact becomes significant when thousands of employees perform the same process repeatedly.

  1. Embedded Self-Service Support

One of the biggest hidden costs in HR transformation projects is support dependency.

Employees continuously ask:

  • “Where do I find this?”
  • “How do I complete this?”
  • “Why is this process failing?”

WalkMe reduces this dependency by bringing answers directly into the application.

Users solve problems themselves instead of waiting for support teams.

  1. User Behaviour Analytics

Most organizations do not truly know where users struggle.

WalkMe provides visibility into:

  • confusing screens,
  • abandoned processes,
  • frequent user errors,
  • adoption gaps,
  • and usability bottlenecks.

This allows organizations to improve processes based on actual user behaviour rather than assumptions.

Why WalkMe Matters Specifically for SAP SuccessFactors

SAP SuccessFactors is feature-rich and highly configurable.
That flexibility is powerful — but it also creates complexity.

Different organizations configure:

  • custom workflows,
  • approval structures,
  • business rules,
  • role permissions,
  • forms,
  • and UI experiences differently.

As a result, standard training materials often fail because every customer environment behaves differently.

WalkMe solves this by delivering guidance tailored to the organization’s actual configuration.

Instead of generic learning, users receive process-specific guidance aligned to their company’s setup.

That is a major advantage.

The Hidden Business Benefits Customers Often Overlook

  • Most companies evaluate WalkMe only from a training perspective.
  • But the larger value is operational.

Faster Stabilization After Go-Live

  • Organizations recover faster after implementation because users become productive sooner.

Reduced HR and IT Burnout

  • Support teams spend less time answering repetitive questions.
  • This improves operational efficiency and reduces frustration internally.

Better Employee Experience

  • Employees feel more comfortable using systems that guide them naturally.
  • Confidence increases. Resistance decreases.

Higher Adoption of Self-Service

  • Many organizations invest in self-service capabilities but employees avoid using them because they feel complicated.
  • WalkMe helps users trust the system.

Greater Return on SAP Investment

  • A system only delivers value when people actually use it effectively.
  • WalkMe increases the likelihood that organizations fully utilize the capabilities they already paid for.

Why Implementation Consultants Benefit Enormously from WalkMe

Consultants are often measured by:

  • adoption success,
  • stabilization speed,
  • user satisfaction,
  • and support effort after go-live.

WalkMe directly improves all four.

Hypercare Becomes More Manageable

Instead of repeatedly answering identical questions, consultants can focus on strategic support and issue resolution.

Change Management Improves Dramatically

WalkMe reduces user anxiety because guidance exists directly inside the process.

This makes transitions smoother during:

  • deployments,
  • enhancements,
  • acquisitions,
  • and quarterly releases.

Training Effort Reduces Over Time

Consultants spend less time producing massive documentation libraries that users rarely read.

Consultants Become More Strategic

Instead of acting as support agents, consultants can focus on:

  • process optimization,
  • digital transformation,
  • UX improvements,
  • and adoption strategy.

This increases consulting value significantly.

What Happens When Organizations Ignore Digital Adoption?

  • Many companies underestimate the long-term cost of poor adoption.
  • Without platforms like WalkMe, organizations often experience:

Continuous Dependency on Support Teams

The same questions repeat endlessly across HR and IT.

Low Utilization of System Features

Employees use only basic functionality while advanced capabilities remain untouched.

Frustration with System Changes

Even small UI updates create confusion when users lack guidance.

Poor Data Quality

Incorrect entries create downstream operational problems:

  • reporting issues,
  • workflow failures,
  • compliance risks,
  • and payroll errors.

Slower Digital Transformation

  • Technology alone does not transform organizations.
  • People do.
  • And if people struggle to use the technology, transformation slows down significantly.

Final Thoughts

  • The future of enterprise software is no longer just about functionality.
    It is about usability, adoption, and employee experience.
  • Organizations can invest millions in SAP SuccessFactors, but without proper user enablement, much of that investment remains underutilized.
  • WalkMe addresses the most overlooked part of digital transformation:
    the human side of technology.
  • It helps users succeed inside the application, reduces organizational friction, and transforms enterprise systems into experiences that employees can actually navigate confidently.
  • In many ways, WalkMe is not an optional enhancement anymore.
  • It is becoming the operational bridge between implementation and real business value.

entry.title: Re: Reading data from cold store deteriorates performance
entry.content:
Hi

In general its expected, that reading from the COLD store is slower than reading from a table, that lies within HANA.

Also note, that it makes not much sense to put regularly accessed data into the cold store. Only data should be moved into the cold store, that is accessed sporadically.

This is something you have to check with your local HANA and SAP IQ database administrators to see if there is any way to improve the performance.

Also check the document: https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/bwplaolap/3361384020.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=LATEST

 

br

Roland

If the base service is SADL based, this is perhaps the only viable alternative.

entry.title: Re: Designing Value Help Services for the Situation Handling - Extended Framework
entry.content:
Hi @yoppie_aries 

no Value Helps are not mandatory, but they are extremely useful, when 
1) a key users tries to configure a Filter for a Situation Type (or Template). Without value helps, they'd need to know what are valid values to enter
2) a end user when they are using the filters in the My Situation - Extended app

If you are new to Situation Handling, you can very well live without defining proper value helps, but I you want to create a use case that 1) might be adopted from the template a lot and 2) end user need to filter the results in the MySituation app, e.g. when there are many results, value helps are very useful to prevent issue. 

- Axel

entry.title: SAP Subscription Billing - Allowance Management with Provider Contract
entry.content:
Hi SAP Experts,

We are currently evaluating SAP Subscription Billing for a prepaid, usage-based commercial model (credit / allowance package consumption) integrated with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition via Provider Contract.

During our assessment, we identified several limitations and would appreciate guidance on SAP best practices and recommended .

Current Findings

1. Allowances and Provider Contract

Based on SAP Help documentation, allowances are not supported with integrations using Provider Contract (e.g., S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition).

Reference:
https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/feature-overview/allowances

We also observed that allowance-related billing is not transferred to EBDR.

2. Aggregated Usage Requirement

Allowances only support aggregated usage records. Individual usage records are not considered for allowance utilization.

Reference:
https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/feature-overview/allowance-determination-and-allocation?q=allowance+usage

3. Billing Cycle Restriction

Cross billing cycles are not allowed:

  • Calendar → Calendar
  • Anniversary → Anniversary

Reference:
https://help.sap.com/docs/subscription-billing/feature-overview/specification-of-billing-cycles

Our Use Case

We want to support:

  • prepaid credit packages,
  • allowance top-ups,
  • real-time or near real-time consumption,
  • overage charging,
  • threshold notifications (e.g., 90% consumed),
  • integration with S/4HANA Cloud billing and revenue recognition.

Example:

  • customer purchases 100 credits,
  • usage deducts credits,
  • once credits reach zero, either:
    • customer buys additional package, or
    • overage billing starts.

Questions

  1. Why are allowance-related charges not transferred to EBDR? Is there a recommended workaround to transferr allowance rate elements to EBDRs (i.e. SAP Integration Suite (iFlow), direct integration)
  2. Can we top-up allowances to extend an existing balance, or must they always create separate allowances?
  3. If multiple allowances exist, how are threshold notifications and consumption priority handled?
  4. How can we handle cross billing ( i.e. the main subscription is anniversary billed, but allowance/usage charging needs monthly calendar billing?)

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: How to display only existing dimension combination only in SAC excel add-in?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

 

I’m facing an issue related to data combinations and would appreciate any advice.

In my case, I have the following dimensions:

  • Product Category: 20 values
  • Product Type: 70 values
  • Material: 400 values

If no rules are applied, I'll get 20 * 70 * 400 = 560,000 rows of results. However, only about 500 rows are actually valid and maintained in the business context. The data will be displayed by month. With 12 months, the total would be 560,000 × 12, which seems too large.

 

I tried to define some validation rules in the model, and they seem to work fine for display purposes when I select the “Hide unplannable and unbooked data” option and restrict the product category to a small set. 

However, when I open the full data in the Excel Add-In, I’m not able to open the table. Instead, I get the following error message:

“The data can’t be displayed because too many records are returned. Please try to reduce the amount of data by collapsing hierarchies, removing dimensions, or applying filters.”

I’m not sure why this error occurs. Does this mean that Excel is still counting the cells that are hidden by the hide unplannable unbooked data option when retrieving the data?

I’m also unsure whether using validation rules is the right approach to control and display valid combinations across three dimensions. Does anyone have a better approach or best practice for modeling or displaying only valid dimension combinations in this scenario?

Any suggestions or insights would be greatly appreciated. Thanks in advance!

(I attached a screenshot for illustration.)

SAP Analytics Cloud

entry.title: Extend RAP based app - Manage sales quotation version 2 app
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

My goal is to add the standard fields VBAK-KVGR1 and VBAK-KVGR2 to the Manage Sales Quotation (Version 2) app, specifically in the filter bar and the report output.

I initially attempted this via Key User Extensibility, but since this is a RAP-based application, the Custom Fields and Logic (data source extension) approach does not seem to be applicable.

I then proceeded with Developer Extensibility (even though cloud extensibility is marked as 'No') by extending the underlying projection view R_SalesQuotationTP, which is used by the main consumption view C_SalesQuotationManageTP. The required fields (KVGR1, KVGR2) are currently visible in R_SalesQuotationTP, but they are not propagated to C_SalesQuotationManageTP.

I am unable to extend C_SalesQuotationManageTP further, as it is a projection view and adding associations is not permitted at this layer.

Additionally, I created a metadata extension to expose these fields as selection fields on the UI. However, the new fields are still not appearing in the application.

I would like to understand:

  • Am I missing any steps in making these fields available end-to-end?
  • Is there any required step to refresh or regenerate the service binding / metadata beyond cache invalidation (which I have already performed)?
  • What is the recommended approach to extend this standard RAP application with these standard fields?

Any guidance or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your help.

entry.title: Issue in Direct GRN automatic PO Net price
entry.content:
Hello Consultants, 

Recently, we have encountered an issue regarding direct GRN's automatic Purchase order fetches the previous PO's net price instead of the updated price for the random materials (not all materials). 

So, it's a sequential updating process. First, we update the info record using BAPI 'ME_INFORECORD_MAINTAIN_MULTI', and we include it in the wrapper function module (RFC). Second, we are posting GRN (101) using BAPI 'BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE'.  

The whole process runs in the OData api and it has been integrated with an external third-party application.  We post the goods movement (using above mentioned BAPIs) in SAP once we receive the payload data from the external system. 

These two BAPIs are executed sequentially per post call triggered from the external system. Hence, we used a wrapper function module for info record update, and cross check the info record and condition price related database tables (EINE, A067, and KONP) after execution of the info record update BAPI.  Now, we have two scenarios as follows,

i) Sometimes the database tables didn't update with the correct price for random materials, even though we used transaction commit BAPI, and wait upto 10 secs logic.

ii) Sometimes the database is updated with the correct price. However, the GRN BAPI auto PO picks the previous day's net price for random materials. 

Note: There are no custom enhancements at the backend, and the configurations have been checked along with MM consultants.

I am looking forward to your responses.

Many thanks in advance!

entry.title: Usage of Data Store and its Impact
entry.content:
We have a requirement to send latest data once a day from MDG to Receiver and we have few approach to fulfill the same. We are looking for best solution/suggestion.

We have a requirement to send Material master data (latest) from MDG via DRF to Receiver daily once and we have below 3 approach

1) MDG is already sending data from MDG using DRF in real time to CPI, CPI is sending to AEM and using other iflow, CPI is picking data from AEM and sending to receiver.
But as our requirement is to send data in batches once a day, so in this case, MDG sends data in batches once a day to CPI via DRF and CPI sends to AEM, from AEM, CPI again picks data and send to receiver.
As we dont have picking data from AEM (based on some schedule), so we have come up with approach.

2) As MDG already sending data to CPI in real time, so we can create another queue, pick from AEM and then send data to Datastore (overwrite the records based on key field) and then using another iflows, with timer/scheduler, CPI can pick all data from data store and send to receiver.
3) Use the same approach as option 2 and instead of using Data store, we can use other external database like datalake, etc.

Can you please confirm which one can be best suited here ? and any pros and cons of using DataStore (where we have to store data for 24 hours) or any other recommendation in a case where CPI has to pick data from AEM and send in batches to receiver ?

Regards,
Saurav Kumar

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: SAP Community Speakers Series and How AI Simplified my Blogging
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

Today is a very exciting day, as SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando is here, and we're announcing our 2026 SAP Sapphire speakers' series featuring: @PrakashNanduri  @PeterSpielvogel @NoorNaqvi97 @kpsauer @KaraReed @Daniel_Dukes @sudhakarsingh @Shaheen_Umer 

As you can see by looking at the SAP Sapphire catalog, AI and the innovations it brings are going to be a major focus. Before diving into the great blog posts by our community members, I want to talk about how AI innovations impact my daily work:

  • Upgraded Simplified Blogging methodology (AI-based)
  • How Simplified Blogging helps me combine my love for long walks and creating blog posts in no time
  • Personal branding in the era of AI and SAP Community contributions to it

Upgraded Simplified Blogging

Last May, I presented the concept of simplified blogging methodology. Currently, the methodology mainly targets event-related blog posts (by speakers and attendees), but later on it will be expanded to other topics as well. Today, I will highlight its foundations and recent improvements that enable you to quickly craft a high-quality blog post while maintaining your authentic tone and voice.

Simplified Blogging step by step

Before following these steps, please be sure to complete the SAP Community tutorial as it's required for blogging on the SAP Community and offers a lot of valuable tips..

1) Template/draft creation 

List and structure the topics you would like to cover in a clear template/draft. Select a distinct title for each paragraph/section (for this example I used a blog post about simplified methodology).

Here is a template for example based on the blog post you are reading now:

  • Introduction
  • Upgraded Simplified Blogging
  • Simplified Blogging step by step (you can create a preset title for each step)
  • Personal Branding in the Era of AI
  • SAP Sapphire 2026 speakers' series!
  • Call to action

You can remove the sections' titles that don't follow your flow and just keep the paragraph's text (like in the introduction and conclusion section in this post where they don't a have paragraph title).

2) Record yourself and generate a transcript 

Today, many applications offer the ability to record and create a transcript. Use the application that you usually use for meetings, but this time have the meeting with yourself :). Record yourself with the transcript option turned on, and before speaking about each topic, say the topic name and then talk about it. This will help the AI tool separate the different sections in the blog post based on your transcript. 

I love having these meetings where I record myself during my evening or morning walks. I believe those are the times when I'm most energized and creative. The work on this blog post was mainly done during a morning walk, as you can see in the picture below :). By they way in the background you can see one of the grapevines, among other amazing fruit trees, that make every walk tasty and naturally energized.

During my morning walk while practicing the upgraded Simplified Blogging Methodology and being happy about it:)

3) Transform the transcript into a template

Go to the AI tool you normally use and apply the following steps/prompts:

Write: "Please embed this transcript [paste transcript] into this template [paste template] while maintaining the original words and ensuring the grammar is correct." Then, you should get a filled template ready for review and improvement. There are better chances for this to work if, when recording, you said the topics out loud according to the template.

I also encourage you to experiment on your own and share in the comments section what worked best for you with your tool..

4) Review, validate and modify/improve as needed

Review the post, validate and ensure it reflects accurately what you wish to communicate.

5) Final grammar review

After you finish making your edits, you can ask the AI tool to give it another grammar and spelling check. This way you can ensure you're ready for publication.

6) Publish

Once you feel your post is ready and follows all relevant guidelines, publish it where you think it fits best as explained in section in the SAP Community tutorial. Please also ensure to follow our AI content guidelines  and the SAP Community ROE .

Personal Branding in the Era of AI

Another interesting aspect is how people are being perceived and recognized in the era of AI tools. A couple of weeks ago, I went into one of the AI tools and I asked "Who is Moshe Naveh from SAP?". I was astonished by the answer it gave me. It summarized my path since I joined 16 years ago. It covered my achievements, what I do and a lot more.

I then asked it, "How did you define this?" It replied, "I inferred it from publicly available SAP Community pages...". So, I recommend that everyone who wants to establish their online branding in the SAP world publish in the SAP Community. 

I continued the interesting conversation with the AI tool by asking other questions, such as why it chose to focus on the SAP Community. It also later gave me tips on how to improve my online visibility, and the topic for this post is part of that coaching.

I recommend that all of you enhance your online branding and become active on the SAP Community by:

1) Actively engage in discussions and questions 

2) Publish blog posts

3) Participate in different initiatives like Hello SAP Community, Hobbies series, events series and more 

Now, like with every great tool and capability, our SAP Community members can choose how to use AI in the way that best suits them and reflects their message. Some of our speakers preferred to write their posts in the old-fashioned way, while others used different AI-based methods, and they all created great blog posts! 

SAP Sapphire 2026 speakers' series!

Now, let's dive into the great blog posts written by our SAP Community members about their exciting SAP Sapphire sessions. These blog posts are a great way to get to know the SAP Community members who are going to speak at SAP Sapphire, learn about their topics, and engage with them through the interesting questions they ask the community to answer.

 

Speaker (role @ SAP)

Blog post

(Global head of Product Management)

Getting to know data products in SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: Re: Unable to fill in Quantity in Purchase Order Price Un...
entry.content:
Good day @Iris59 

Thank you for your question.

You cannot fill the field Quantity in Purchase Order Price unit through the released Material Document API if it is not provided in the API metadata.

The supported approach for PO-based goods receipt, is for you to pass the goods receipt quantity using QuantityInEntryUnit and EntryUnit, together with the purchase order reference, purchase order item, movement type, plant, storage location, and batch where you deem it relevant. The quantity in PO price unit is then driven by the system based on the PO item, unit conversion, batch-specific UoM setup, and valuation logic.

Here is what I recommend:

  1. Do not add the field manually to the payload, because unreleased fields are not supported by the API contract.
  2. You should make use of QuantityInEntryUnit and EntryUnit in the goods receipt API payload.
  3. Check the purchase order item setup for order unit order unit, order price unit, price unit, and material UoM conversion. 
  4. Fix the original Batch-Specific Unit of Measure validation issue rather than using the PO price unit quantity as a workaround.
  5. If this field is required as an API input for your business process, consider raising an influence request. Influence Opportunity Homepage - Customer Influence

Here are references for more guidance

3352378 - Sample payload for API API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV to post movement type 161 for blocked stock - SAP for Me

3027233 - sample payload for API API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV to post movement type 262 - SAP for Me

362932 - Conversion with proportion/product units - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How to Create Multiple intervals in the multi shift d...
entry.content:
Hi,

you can add time-intervals in the valuation section of the "Manage Fixed Asset" app. If the multiple-shift indicator changes every month, you can create a new time-interval starting at the first day of the depreciation period and store the new multiple-shift value.

Kind Regards,

Markus
